behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive/subdirectory event delivery), which depends on OS-level watcher semantics. A mocked watcher can hide real misses where subdirectory events are not emitted. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: this is filesystem watching only, with no AI/ML call and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon watching a temporary root directory, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert the transformed output appears in the output directory exactly once within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is primarily module handoff/state coordination (request intake + dedupe window + pipeline trigger) and can be caught in-process with real collaborating modules plus fake clock/time control. System was considered and rejected because behavior text is about duplicate requests for the same path/window, not OS-specific watcher quirks. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified: no AI/ML output dependency or multi-agent chain is described.
behaviors.test_description: Wire intake, dedupe store, and processing trigger together in-process; submit two requests for the same path within the configured window; assert only one processing invocation occurs; advance clock past window and assert a new request is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly names OS-emitted duplicate filesystem events; that failure appears at real OS watcher boundaries and can be masked by mocked event streams. Integration is too low because in-process fakes may not reproduce real duplicate event patterns/timing. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text: no model call or agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon against a real temp directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple fs events (e.g., create+modify/rename), record processing invocations, and assert exactly one completed processing for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory deletion/recreation while running depends on real OS filesystem and watcher reattachment semantics (inode/path lifecycle). Mocks can miss failures where the watcher remains attached to a dead handle. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: this is OS/filesystem resiliency, not AI/ML behavior and not multi-step agentic reasoning.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real temp directory, delete the directory, recreate it at the same path, then add a new file; assert the daemon processes the new file without daemon restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: The core risk is ordering logic across local modules (event intake, metadata lookup, queue ordering, processor dispatch). This can be reproduced in-process by injecting same-arrival requests with controlled timestamps and asserting dispatch order. System was considered and rejected because behavior text focuses on ordering rule by timestamps, not explicitly on OS-specific event delivery failure. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified: no AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run watcher-to-queue-to-processor modules together in-process; inject concurrent arrival events for multiple files with controlled creation timestamps; assert processing order follows timestamp order regardless of event arrival order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real filesystem write timing/visibility and event timing races. In-process mocks often hide partial-write states and lock/flush behavior, so MOCK-HIDE pushes to System. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no model output quality or multi-agent steps are involved.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp directory, write a large file in chunks with delays while keeping write stream open, verify processing does not start before stream close/completion, then verify processing occurs after completion.
